steps.title: Identify how Text 2 acknowledges the principle
steps.content:
The strongest signal is Text 2's explicit statement: “Many other predation relief studies show an increase in prey abundance.” The author is confirming the predator-control principle holds in most cases.

Yet their own study found something different—predator exclusion had no effect on deer.

Rather than rejecting the principle, they explain the discrepancy: the principle works for small, rapidly reproducing prey but not necessarily for large, slowly reproducing prey.

steps.title: Match this conditional support to the answer
steps.content:
The author accepts the principle as having genuine evidential backing but recognizes it doesn't apply universally—it depends on prey characteristics.

Choice D states exactly this: the principle has evidence supporting it but should not be regarded as universally applicable.

The other three choices introduce issues not discussed in Text 2: methodological flaws in the supporting studies, the acceptance status of the Gulsby findings, or predator characteristics rather than prey variation.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
